title: Listening - Part 3: The Nostalgia Trap
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a sociologist, Noah, and a marketing consultant, Sonia, about why modern culture is obsessed with the past. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring the 'Golden Age' fallacy and how brands use memory to drive consumption.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: It seems we can’t look forward without first looking back. From film remakes to vintage fashion, nostalgia is everywhere. Joining me are Noah, a sociologist, and Sonia, who advises brands on consumer trends. Noah, why are we so anchored in the past right now?

Noah: [15] Nostalgia was once considered a medical disease—a form of melancholy. Today, we see it as a psychological 'buffer.' In times of intense social or technological upheaval, we retreat to a version of the past that feels stable and predictable. [/15] It’s not about the past as it actually was, but about a sanitized, 'comfy' version of it. We use these memories to ground our identity when the future feels like it's moving too fast to track.

Interviewer: Sonia, the business world has certainly noticed. Is 'nostalgia marketing' just a lazy way to sell products?

Sonia: [16] Lazy? Perhaps. Effective? Incredibly. When a brand brings back a logo or a product from the 90s, they aren't just selling an object; they are selling a feeling of safety. It bypasses the rational brain and goes straight to the limbic system. [/16] For Gen Z, who didn't even experience the 90s, it’s 'anemoia'—nostalgia for a time you never knew. They see it as an era of 'authenticity' before the internet made everything so complicated. It’s a very powerful tool for building instant trust.

Interviewer: Noah, is there a danger in this? Can a society be too focused on its highlights reel?

Noah: [17] The risk is 'cultural stagnation.' If we are always looking back, we stop innovating. We see this in Hollywood, where studios would rather fund a safe sequel than a risky original idea. [/17] But more importantly, nostalgia can be weaponized politically. The idea of a 'Golden Age' is almost always a myth that ignores the struggles of that time. When we romanticize the past, we often end up justifying the prejudices of the past as well.

Interviewer: Sonia, how do you keep nostalgia fresh without it becoming repetitive?

Sonia: [18] The key is 'Newstalgia'—taking something familiar and giving it a modern, high-tech twist. It’s about the intersection of the 'comforting old' and the 'exciting new.' [/18] Think of electric cars that look like vintage models. You get the emotional connection of the classic design but with the ethics and efficiency of modern technology. That’s the sweet spot for the modern consumer.

Interviewer: Noah, you’ve mentioned that nostalgia is actually a very 'social' emotion. How so?

Noah: [19] It’s a way of saying 'I belong.' When we share a nostalgic reference—a song, a toy, a TV show—we are reinforcing a shared cultural script. It bridges the gap between generations. [/19] It’s one of the few things that can create a sense of community in a very individualized, digital world. It’s a 'social glue.'

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Sonia? Is the nostalgia boom going to end?

Sonia: [20] Not as long as the world remains unpredictable. As long as there is anxiety about the future, there will be a market for the past. We are 'sentimental animals,' and that isn't going to change with an algorithm. [/20]

Interviewer: Noah, Sonia, thank you.

questions.question: Noah suggests that people use nostalgia today to
options: A) escape from the physical symptoms of a medical disease.
B) provide emotional stability during periods of rapid change.
C) accurately record the history of their own families.
D) avoid making important decisions about their professional lives.
questions.explanation: He calls nostalgia a 'psychological buffer' used to retreat to a version of the past that feels 'stable and predictable' during upheaval.

questions.question: According to Sonia, why does nostalgia marketing work so well?
options: A) It appeals to the customer's logic and need for efficiency.
B) It creates a sense of emotional security that is hard to resist.
C) It is cheaper for companies to use old designs than new ones.
D) Younger generations are tired of using high-tech products.
questions.explanation: She says brands are selling a 'feeling of safety' that bypasses the rational brain and goes straight to the emotional limbic system.

questions.question: What is the 'cultural' danger of nostalgia that Noah describes?
options: A) It makes young people lose respect for their elders.
B) It prevents society from coming up with new and original ideas.
C) It causes people to spend too much money on vintage items.
D) It makes filmmakers stop using the latest technology.
questions.explanation: He warns of 'cultural stagnation' where studios and society prefer safe sequels over risky original ideas.

questions.question: The concept of 'Newstalgia' involves
options: A) rejecting all modern technology in favor of traditional methods.
B) ignoring the past and focusing only on future innovations.
0) combining classic, familiar aesthetics with modern functionality.
D) trying to predict which current products will be nostalgic in the future.
questions.explanation: She describes it as taking something familiar and giving it a 'high-tech twist', like an electric car with a vintage design.

questions.question: Noah believes that sharing nostalgic memories can
options: A) create conflict between different age groups.
B) help to create a sense of belonging and community.
C) make people realize how much they have wasted their lives.
D) encourage people to stop using social media.
questions.explanation: He calls it a 'social glue' that reinforces a shared cultural script and bridges the gap between generations.

questions.question: Sonia concludes that the demand for nostalgic products will continue as long as
options: A) the internet continues to grow at its current rate.
B) companies can keep their prices low enough for everyone.
C) people feel uncertain about what the future holds.
D) there are no new inventions to replace the old ones.
questions.explanation: She states that as long as there is 'anxiety about the future', there will be a market for the past.
